description: Medical figures and diagrams extracted from source PDFs

schema.page: integer – page number in source file
schema.local_path: string – (optional) path to saved image file

images.description: FIGURE 10-2 The chain of infection. GI, gastrointestinal; GU, genitourinary.
Image of a chemical thermometer.
Image of a tympanic thermometer.
FIGURE 10-8 An autoclave. (Photo by B. Proud.)

FIGURE 19-7 Evacuation of clients. (A) Human crutches—rescuers secure a weak but ambulatory client's arm and waist. (B) Seat carry—rescuers interlock arms and carry a nonambulatory client. (C) Body drag—rescuers drag an unconscious victim or one who cannot assist on a blanket or sheet.
FIGURE 12-1 The hypothalamus regulates body temperature.
FIGURE 12-11 Phases of a fever and physiologic changes.
FIGURE 12-12 The peripheral pulse sites.
FIGURE 12-17 An aneroid (A) and an electronic oscillometric manometer (B).
FIGURE 12-18 To determine the appropriate size of blood pressure cuff, the width of the bladder should be 40% of the midarm circumference and the length should be at least 80%.
FIGURE 12-22 A stethoscope (A) and a chest piece (B).

FIGURE 12-20 Characteristics of Korotkoff sounds showing progression from 150 mm Hg to 0 mm Hg with phases: Phase I faint tapping, Phase II swishing, Phase III loud knocking, Phase IV muffled, Phase V silence.
FIGURE 17-1 A cross-section of the skin.
FIGURE 17-4 A cross-section of a tooth. (From Cohen, B. [2010]. Medical terminology: An illustrated guide [4th ed.]. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.)
FIGURE 9-3 Sample of PIE charting.
FIGURE 9-4 Portable computers allow for point of care documentation. (From Craven, R.F., Hirnle, C.J. Fundamentals of Nursing [6th ed.]. Philadelphia: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.)
FIGURE 9-6 The military clock uses one 24-hour time cycle instead of two 12-hour cycles (eg, 9:00 AM is 0900 and 9:00 PM is 2100).
FIGURE 23-1 Good posture helps to align gravity through the center of the body. A wide stance provides a stable base for support.

FIGURE 23-2 (A) A good standing posture results when abdominal and gluteal muscles are contracted. (B) A poor standing posture results when abdominal muscles are relaxed, causing altered body alignment.

FIGURE 23-3 (A) A correct sitting posture. (B) An incorrect sitting posture. (From Hendrickson, T. [2010]. Massage and manual therapy for orthopedic conditions [2nd ed.]. Philadelphia, PA: Wolters Kluwer Health and Pharma.)
FIGURE 23-4 (A) Maintaining the spine in neutral position (top) facilitates the best lying posture. (B) Positions that create neck flexion (middle) or (C) hyperextension (bottom) are undesirable.
Figure 23-6: A) Supine position, B) Lateral position
Image of a client in prone position.
E) Sims' position
Image showing low Fowler's (30 degrees), semi-Fowler's (45 degrees), and high Fowler's (60-90 degrees) positions.
Diagram showing various positioning devices and body positions.
FIGURE 23-16 A roller sheet is used for turning, moving, and repositioning.
FIGURE 23-12 A hand roll. (Photo by B. Proud.)
FIGURE 23-13 Protective boots to avoid foot drop. (Photo by B. Proud.)
FIGURE 23-21 Using a trapeze to facilitate movement. (From Taylor, C., Lillis, C., & Lynn, P. [2015]. Fundamentals of nursing [8th ed.]. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.)
FIGURE 23-22 Using side rails to prepare for ambulation and to change position.
FIGURE 23-16 An alternating air mattress. (First Step Plus; Courtesy of KCI Therapeutic Services, San Antonio, TX.)
